title: Chermoula sea bass
subtitle: Baked sea bass with North African herb marinade
excerpt: Sea bass fillets marinated in chermoula (a Moroccan herb paste of coriander, parsley, garlic, cumin, and lemon), then roasted until fragrant and just cooked through. One of the best things to do with white fish. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Chermoula is the foundational marinade of Moroccan fish cooking, used to flavour and preserve fish before grilling or baking. Documented by Paula Wolfert in Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco (1973) and Claudia Roden in The Food of the Eastern Mediterranean. The combination of coriander, parsley, garlic, and cumin is consistent; some versions add preserved lemon.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chermoula can be made up to two days ahead. Marinate the fish for up to 30 minutes; longer makes the texture chalky from the lemon.

recipeTools.notes: For the chermoula. A pestle and mortar works well too.

content.text: Chermoula is the Moroccan answer to the question of what to do with white fish. The paste is bold and fragrant — coriander and parsley forward, with garlic, cumin, and enough chilli to give it some heat. It goes on the fish and the fish goes into a very hot oven for 10–12 minutes. That is the whole recipe.

Blitz {{coriander}} of coriander, {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, the lemon juice and zest, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a food processor to a rough paste. Taste and adjust. Place the sea bass fillets in a shallow dish and spoon over half the chermoula. Turn to coat and leave to marinate for 20–30 minutes. Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the fish skin-side down on a lightly oiled roasting tray. Roast for 10–12 minutes until the flesh is just opaque and lifts easily from the skin. Serve with the remaining chermoula spooned alongside, with couscous or flatbread.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough, so the fish cooked slowly rather than fast. Or fillet too thin.
items.fix: The oven must be fully preheated to 220°C before the fish goes in. At this temperature, a 150 g fillet takes exactly 10–12 minutes. Check at 10 by pressing the thickest part — it should feel just firm.

items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or coarse stalks included.
items.fix: Use only the fine stems of the coriander, not the thick lower stalks. Start with the juice of half a lemon and add more to taste.
Add olive oil a tablespoon at a time until the paste loosens to a spreadable consistency.

content.text: Chermoula is to Moroccan fish cooking what sofrito is to Spanish cooking — a foundational paste that goes on almost everything. It is used as a marinade for fish before grilling or baking, as a dipping sauce, and as a flavouring for couscous and vegetables. Sea bass takes it well because the mild, clean flesh does not compete with the assertive herb flavour. The paste is also excellent with sea bream, cod, or any firm white fish. In Morocco it would typically be used with whole fish rather than fillets, the paste rubbed into the slashed flesh before grilling over charcoal.
